sections.label: Section 728
sections.original: ἰὼ , ἰώ , δαίμονος τύχα βαρεῖα . φεῦ φεῦ .
sections.translation: Oh , oh ! Cruel stroke of fate . Woe , woe !

sections.label: Section 729
sections.translation: Hush ! be silent all , crouch low ; for perhaps there comes someone into the snare .

sections.label: Section 731
sections.translation: Oh , oh ! dire mishap to the Thracians .

sections.label: Section 732b
sections.translation: It is one of the allies who is groaning .

sections.label: Section 733
sections.translation: Oh , oh ! woe to me and to you , O king of Thrace , how cursed the sight of Troy to you !

sections.label: Section 735
sections.translation: what an end to your life !

sections.label: Section 736
sections.original: τίς εἶ ποτʼ ἀνδρῶν συμμάχων ; κατʼ εὐφρόνην
sections.translation: Who are you ? One of the allies ? Night ’ s gloom has dulled these eyes and I cannot clearly recognize you .

sections.label: Section 738
sections.translation: Where can I find some Trojan chief ? Where does Hector

sections.label: Section 740
sections.translation: take his rest under arms ? To which of the captains of the army am I to tell my tale ? What sufferings ours ! what dark deeds someone has wrought on us and gone his way , when he had wound up a ball of sorrow manifest to every Thracian !

sections.label: Section 745
sections.translation: From what I gather of this man ’ s words , some calamity , it seems , is befalling the Thracian army .

sections.label: Section 747
sections.translation: Lost is all our army , our prince is dead , slain by a treacherous blow . Oh ! Oh !

sections.label: Section 750
sections.translation: The cruel anguish of this bloody wound that racks my frame within ! Would I were dead ! Was it to die this inglorious death that Rhesus and I came bringing aid to Troy ?

sections.label: Section 754
sections.original: τάδʼ οὐκ ἐν αἰνιγμοῖσι σημαίνει κακά ·
sections.translation: He does not declare the disaster in riddles ;

sections.label: Section 755
sections.translation: no , he clearly asserts our allies ’ destruction .

sections.label: Section 756
sections.original: κακῶς πέπρακται κἀπὶ τοῖς κακοῖσι πρὸς
sections.translation: A sorry deed it was , and more than that a deed most shameful ; yes , it is an evil doubly bad ; to die with glory , if die one must , is bitterness enough I think to him who dies — how not ?—

sections.label: Section 760
sections.original: τοῖς ζῶσι δʼ ὄγκος καὶ δόμων εὐδοξία .
sections.translation: though to the living it may add dignity and honor for their house . But we have died foolishly and without glory . No sooner had great Hector given us our quarters and told us the password than we lay down to sleep upon the ground , overcome by weariness . No guard our army set

sections.label: Section 765
sections.translation: to watch by night , nor were our arms set in array , nor were the whips hung ready on the horses ’ yokes , for our prince was told that you were masters now , and had encamped hard on their ships ; so carelessly we threw ourselves down to sleep .

sections.label: Section 770
sections.translation: Now I with thoughtful mind awoke from my slumber , and with ungrudging hand measured out the horses ’ feed , expecting to harness them at dawn for the fray ; through the thick gloom two men I see roaming around our army . But when I roused myself

sections.label: Section 775
sections.translation: they crouched low and were gone once more ; and I called out to them to keep away from the army , for I thought they might be thieves from our allies . Nothing from them , so I too said no more , but came back to my couch and slept again .

sections.label: Section 780
sections.original: καί μοι καθʼ ὕπνον δόξα τις παρίσταται ·
sections.translation: And as I slept a strange fancy came over me : those horses that I had reared and used to drive , stationed at Rhesus ’ side , I saw , I thought as in a dream , with wolves mounted on their backs ; and these with their tails lashed the horses ’ flanks

sections.label: Section 785
sections.original: ἤλαυνον , αἳ δʼ ἔρρεγκον ἐξ ἀντηρίδων
sections.translation: and urged them on , while they snorted and breathed fury from their nostrils , striving in terror to unseat their riders . Up I sprang to defend the horses from the brutes , for the horror of the night scared me . Then as I raised my head I heard the groans of dying men ,

sections.label: Section 790
sections.original: θερμὸς δὲ κρουνὸς δεσπότου πάρα σφαγαῖς
sections.translation: and a warm stream of new-shed blood bespattered me , beside my murdered master as he died . To my feet I start , but all unarmed ; and as I peer about and grope to find my sword , a stalwart hand from somewhere near struck me with a sword

sections.label: Section 795
sections.translation: beneath the ribs . I felt the sword-thrust , the deep gaping wound it gave me . Down on my face I fell , while they fled clean away with horses and chariot . Oh , oh ! Tortured with pain , too weak to stand , a piteous object !

sections.label: Section 800
sections.original: καὶ ξυμφορὰν μὲν οἶδʼ ὁρῶν , τρόπῳ δʼ ὅτῳ
sections.translation: I know what happened , for I saw it ; but how the victims met their death I cannot say , nor at whose hand . But I can well surmise we have our friends to thank for this grief .

sections.label: Section 804
sections.translation: Charioteer of Thrace ’ s hapless king ,

sections.label: Section 805
sections.translation: do not suspect us . Enemies did this . But Hector himself is here , having learned your mischance ; he sympathizes as he should with your hard fate .

sections.label: Section 808
sections.translation: You who have caused this great calamity , how did the enemy ’ s spies come

sections.label: Section 810
sections.translation: without your knowledge , to your shame , and spread destruction through the army , and you did not drive them away either as they entered or left the camp ? Who but you shall pay the penalty for this ? You , I say , were here to guard the army . But they are gone without a wound , with many a scoff

sections.label: Section 815
sections.original: πόλλʼ ἐγγελῶντες τῷ στρατηλάτῃ τʼ ἐμοί .
sections.translation: at Phrygian cowardice , and at me their leader . Now mark you this — by father Zeus I swear — either the scourge or the headsman ’ s axe awaits you for such conduct ; or else consider Hector to be nothing , a mere coward .

sections.label: Section 1.820
sections.translation: Woe , woe ! It was in quest of you , yes , you , great lord of my city , that I went , when I brought news to you that the Argive army was kindling fires about the ships ;

sections.label: Section 1.825
sections.translation: for by the springs of Simois I vow my eye kept sleepless watch by night , nor did I slumber or sleep . Do not be angry with me , my lord ; I am guiltless of all .

sections.label: Section 1.830
sections.original: εἰ δὲ χρόνῳ παράκαιρον ἔργον ἢ λόγον
sections.translation: Yet if hereafter you find that I in word or deed have done amiss , bury me alive beneath the earth ; I ask no mercy .

sections.label: Section 1.833
sections.original: τί τοῖσδʼ ἀπειλεῖς βάρβαρός τε βαρβάρου
sections.translation: Why threaten these ? Why try to undermine my barbarian mind with your subtle words , you yourself a barbarian ?

sections.label: Section 1.835
sections.original: σὺ ταῦτʼ ἔδρασας · οὐδένʼ ἂν δεξαίμεθα
sections.translation: You did this deed ; neither they who have died nor we who are wounded will believe it was any other . A long and clever speech you ’ ll need to prove to me you did not slay your friends because you coveted the horses , and to gain them murdered

sections.label: Section 1.840
sections.original: τοὺς σοὺς φονεύεις , πόλλʼ ἐπισκήπτων μολεῖν .
sections.translation: your own allies , after strongly imposing on them come . They came , they are dead ; Paris found more decent means to shame the rights of hospitality than you , who killed your allies . No , do not tell me some Argive came and slaughtered us . Who could have passed the Trojan lines

sections.label: Section 1.845
sections.original: Τρώων ἐφʼ ἡμᾶς ἦλθεν , ὥστε καὶ λαθεῖν ;
sections.translation: and come against us without detection ? You and your Phrygian troops were camped in front of us . Who was wounded , who was slain among your friends , when that foe you speak of came ? It was we , far off , were wounded , while some have met a sterner

sections.label: Section 1.850
sections.translation: fate and said farewell to the sunlight . Briefly , then , I blame no Achaean . For what enemy could have come and found the lowly bed of Rhesus in the dark , unless some god were guiding the murderers ’ steps ? They did not know so much

sections.label: Section 1.855
sections.original: τὸ πάμπαν ᾖσαν · ἀλλὰ μηχανᾷ τάδε .
sections.translation: as know of his arrival . No , this is your plot !

sections.label: Section 1.856
sections.translation: For a long time now I have had to do with allies , yes , ever since Achaea ’ s army settled in this land , and never a harsh word have I known them say of me ; but with you I am to make a beginning . Never may such longing

sections.label: Section 1.860
sections.translation: for horses seize me that I should slay my friends ! This is the work of Odysseus ; for who of all the Argives but he would have devised or carried out such a deed ? I fear him ; and my mind is a little troubled lest he should have met and slain Dolon as well ;

sections.label: Section 1.865
sections.original: χρόνον γὰρ ἤδη φροῦδος ὢν οὐ φαίνεται .
sections.translation: for he has been gone a long time and does not appear .

sections.label: Section 1.866
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδα τοὺς σοὺς οὓς λέγεις Ὀδυσσέας ·
sections.translation: I do not know this Odysseus of whom you speak . It was no enemy ’ s hand that struck us .

sections.label: Section 1.868
sections.original: σὺ δʼ οὖν νόμιζε ταῦτʼ , ἐπείπερ σοι δοκεῖ .
sections.translation: If it pleases you to think that , do so .

sections.label: Section 1.869
sections.original: ὦ γαῖα πατρίς , πῶς ἂν ἐνθάνοιμί σοι ;
sections.translation: O land of my fathers , would I might die in you !

sections.label: Section 1.870
sections.original: μὴ θνῇσχʼ · ἅλις γὰρ τῶν τεθνηκότων ὄχλος .
sections.translation: Die ! No ! Enough are those already dead .

sections.label: Section 1.871
sections.translation: Where am I to turn , I ask you , bereft of my master ?

sections.label: Section 1.872
sections.translation: My house shall shelter you and cure you of your hurt .

sections.label: Section 1.873
sections.original: καὶ πῶς με κηδεύσουσιν αὐθεντῶν χέρες ;
sections.translation: How shall murderers ’ hands care for me ?

sections.label: Section 1.874
sections.original: ὅδʼ αὖ τὸν αὐτὸν μῦθον οὐ λήξει λέγων .
sections.translation: This fellow will never have done repeating the same story .

sections.label: Section 1.875
sections.original: ὄλοιθʼ ὁ δράσας . οὐ γὰρ ἐς σὲ τείνεται
sections.translation: Curses on the doer of this deed ! On you my tongue fixes no charge , as you complain ; but Justice is over all .

sections.label: Section 1.877
sections.original: λάζυσθʼ · ἄγοντες δʼ αὐτὸν ἐς δόμους ἐμούς ,
sections.translation: Take him away ; carry him to my palace and tend him carefully , that he may have no fault to find . And you must go to those upon the walls ,

sections.label: Section 1.880
sections.original: Πριάμῳ τε καὶ γέρουσι σημῆναι νεκροὺς
sections.translation: to Priam and his aged councillors , and tell them to give orders for the burial of the dead at the resting-place along the public road . The charioteer is carried off .

sections.label: Section 882
sections.original: τί ποτʼ εὐτυχίας ἐκ τῆς μεγάλης
sections.translation: Why does fate change and bring Troy once again to mourning after her great good fortune , planting what seeds ?

sections.label: Section 885
sections.translation: Oh , oh ! What deity above our heads , O king , bears in her hands as on a bier the newly slain corpse ? I shudder at this sight of woe .

sections.label: Section 890
sections.original: ὁρᾶν πάρεστι , Τρῶες · ἡ γὰρ ἐν σοφοῖς
sections.translation: Behold me , Trojans ; for I , the Muse , one of the nine sisters , that have honor among the wise , I am here , having seen the piteous death his foes have dealt my darling son . Yet the crafty Odysseus , that slew him , one day hereafter shall pay a fitting penalty .

sections.label: Section 1.895
sections.translation: O my son , your mother ’ s grief , I mourn for you in my native strains of woe ! What a journey you made to Troy , a very path of ill-fortune and sorrow !

sections.label: Section 1.900
sections.translation: starting , in spite of all my warnings and your father ’ s earnest prayers , in defiance of us . Woe to me for you , my dear , dear son ! Ah , woe !

sections.label: Section 1.904
sections.translation: As far as one can who has no common tie of kin ,

sections.label: Section 1.905
sections.original: ἔχοντι λύπῃ τὸν σὸν οἰκτίρω γόνον .
sections.translation: I bewail and mourn your son .

sections.label: Section 1.906
sections.translation: Curses on the son of Oeneus ! Curses on Laertes ’ child ! who has bereft me of my fair son and left me childless ! and on that woman , too ,
